posts.caption_raw:
apply2uic October 5, 2023: "What is your favorite engineering program—and why is it computer science? The short answer: Well, duh! 

Our College of Engineering—ranked 73 out of the nation's top 100 for engineering schools— UIC's Computer Science program is 60th according to U.S. News & World Report. The college's student enrollment increased by 8.3%. With its promising internships (hey, Google!) and impressive research labs, it's no surprise this is one of Chicago's and the nation's top picks! 

Shoutout to @uicwiep 

Visit us for UIC Preview on Monday, October 9th, to learn more. 

#uic #gouic #apply2uic #uicengineering #womeninengineering #goflames #UICCampus #collegesearch #collegerankings #explorechicago 🗣🗣🗣".

posts.caption_raw:
apply2uic September 21, 2023: "UIC is ranked 37th (tie) for campus ethnic diversity! ✨✨

How is this so? Not only are we committed to student ethnic diversity and inclusion, but also our staff's demographic. 

The Bridge to Faculty program, which focuses on transitioning underrepresented postdoctoral scholars of color into faculty roles, celebrated the newest cohort, marking 49 scholars with 31 already UIC professors per UIC Today. 

"Behind every number, there's a person with a name"! And, we're proud of every single one. 

#UIC #illinoisuniversitychicago #campusrankings #publicuniversity  #chicagoexplore
#collegecampus #gouic  #uiccampus #chicagouniversity #goflamesgo".

posts.caption_raw:
"As the first and only Arab American Cultural Center (ArabAmCC) on a college campus in the United States, the ArabAmCC affirms and supports the diverse needs of the Arab and Arab American students, increases awareness about Arab and Arab American and Muslim American cultures and histories, provides skills building and professional support opportunities for students, and strengthens community among students, staff, faculty, and the Chicagoland. 

ArabAmCC organizes public events and workshops under our 
*Ibda'a (Creative Practices), 
*Hiwarat (Public Conversations), 
*Tawasul (Strengthening Connections)
*Wellness programming. 

The Center also provides Da'am (support services) for students navigating the campus and Tamkeen (capacity building) to support their professional and leadership development. 

ArabAmCC organizes an annual Arab American Graduation Hafla to honor and celebrate our students hard work and achievements.

Link to donate is on link tree in bio".

posts.caption_raw:
"As we near the end of the semester, join us in learning about the seven awesome cultural centers at UIC at the Cultural Carnival organized by students in @uic_cada’s ART 190: Socially Engaged Art 🎪

Enjoy free food, live music, face painting, and more! 🍲🎶🖌️

This would be a great opportunity to get in touch with the cultural centers and all the resources they offer before the new Spring semester starts!

This event will be held at Student Center East, 3rd Floor, Dearborn AB next Monday, December 2nd from 11 am-4pm. Stop by anytime!!

At the event students will have access to a doula booth, earplugs, facemasks, and quiet zone to get some studying in too 📚

Thank you to @uic_cada and the students of ART 190 for organizing this event and recognizing the cultural centers! 🎉".

posts.caption_raw:
"🧘 Join the Arab American Cultural Center for a week of destress activities and food to help you finish the semester strong.

The Center will be open late M-Th until 8pm to give you a safe and comfortable space to study and get help from other students and staff. We will also feature a number of programs each day

Chai Monday (Enjoy different ways to prepare Chai/Shay/Tea)

Henna Tuesday (get in line to get henna done by the talented Mona)

Wednesday Game Day (take a break from studying and play some games)

Artsy Thursday (spend that anxious and stress energy doing something creative)

Free Time Friday (no specific plans but plenty of food) - Please note Center will close at 3pm

Contact
Arab American Cultural Center 312-413-3253 arabamcc@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw:
"Do you want to understand the recent attacks on Lebanon?
Do you want to learn about how Lebanon and Palestine are related?
Do you want to hear about how our lives in the U.S. are wrapped up in the lives of people on the ground in Lebanon and Palestine today?

Join the Global Middle East Studies (GMES) and the Arab American Cultural Center @arabamcc at UIC for a teach-in on the historical context of the current war on Lebanon and the long-standing links between freedom struggles in Lebanon, Palestine, and the Arab region more broadly.

We are featuring international experts on Lebanon: Dr. Maya Mikdashi @mayamikdashi , Dr. Munira Khayyat, and Dr. Zeina Zaatari @dr_zee1982".

"🇯🇴 Join the Arab American Cultural Center's Ana Min Hunak, Ana Min Huna (I'm From There, I'm From Here) series of stories of cultural heritage focusing on Jordan. Listen to students share stories about their heritage, cultural practices, and experiences while also viewing various cultural items and artifacts from Jordan.

Food will be served.

Space is accessible by wheelchair.

Contact
Arab American Cultural Center 312-413-3253 arabamcc@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw: "Last Thursday, November 7th, CCUSC staff were honored for their many contributions serving UIC students, faculty, staff, and the Chicago community. Dr. Rosa Cabrera from @uiclcc received the Award of Merit; Dr. Zeina Zaatari from @arabamcc received the Chancellor’s Academic Professional Award; and Alyson Kung from @uicaarcc received the Janice Watkin’s Award. That evening, @uicgsc associate director Moises Villada was also honored with the About Face Theatre’s inaugural Brian Goodman Shooting Star Award! Thank you CCUSC staff for all that you do on campus and beyond! #CCUSC #EmployeeAwards #ThisIsUIC".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join the Equity and Inclusion in Engineering Program at UIC in an engaging conversation with Arab American women engineers and UIC alumnae. Siham Hussein (google), Rund Daoud (Terra Engineering), Safa Alkhatib (ComEd), and Reem Hussein (Northwestern University) will share their personal journeys, challenges, and triumphs in their respective fields. This event will spotlight their diverse experiences and provide insights into navigating the industry, overcoming barriers, and making an impact!

Space is wheelchair accessible

Refreshments will be provided

Contact
Arab American Cultural Center 312-413-3253 arabamcc@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw:
"✨Join the Global Middle East Studies Minor, the Department of History, the Institute for the Humanities, and the Arab American Cultural Center in a film screening and discussion of:

A Feeling Greater than Love  شعور أكبر من الحب

📽️ Screening will be followed with a discussion with the director, Mary Jirmanus Saba.

In her directorial debut, Mary Jirmanus Saba deals with a forgotten revolution, saving from oblivion bloodily suppressed strikes at Lebanese tobacco and Gandour chocolate factories. These events from the 1970s, which held the promise of a popular revolution, and with it women’s emancipation, were erased from collective memory by the country’s civil wars. Rich in archival footage from Lebanon’s militant cinema tradition, the film reconstructs the spirit of that revolt, asking of the past how we might transform the present.🇱🇧

Space is accessible by wheelchair.

Contact

Arab American Cultural Center

 

312-413-3253

 

arabamcc@uic.edu". ‎

posts.caption_raw:
"📣 Calling all UIC Undergraduates! 

Diversity Community Engagement Program (DCEP)
Spring 2025 Internships are available!

📆 Applications Due: Monday, November 25, 2024 by 9am CST
Visit for more information and to apply now! https://go.uic.edu/DCEP

⭐️ What is DCEP?
The Diversity Community Engagement Program (DCEP) is designed to expand students’ career readiness and community engagement skills through an internship hosted by the Office of Diversity and its seven cultural centers and their community partners.

This internship is for one semester and students will dedicate 10 hours per week.
Federal Work Study and DACA students are encouraged to apply.

🧠 More info 
Interns will receive compensation and/or may be eligible to receive academic credit. All UIC undergraduate students are eligible to apply. Students could apply for placement between an On-Campus Internship or Community Placement Internship.

Internships by the Office of Diversity, Equity & Engagement in Spring 2025 at:
🔥 Any one of the seven Centers for Cultural Understanding and Social Change (CCUSC); or the
🔥 Office of Diversity, Equity & Engagement

✅ We hope you take advantage of this unique program!

If you have any questions, please contact Jorge Mena Robles (jmena2@uic.edu)

IDs: All graphics have a photo of students gathering near a bold mural and some plants at the top. All graphics have a UIC Diversity logo and a link to more information along the bottom: go.uic.edu/DCEP
ID 1: DCEP Calling All Undergraduates graphic. A round photo shows three smiling DCEP interns from various Centers, and a red rectangle with a calendar icon emphasizes the deadline, November 25 at 9am.
ID 2: What is DCEP? (information transcribed above)
ID 3: More info about compensation/credit and sites (information transcribed above)
ID 4: We hope you take advantage of this unique program with contact for questions and a red circle emphasizing the deadline with a calendar icon.".

"Ana Min Hunak, Ana Min Huna (I am from there, I am from here) is a series that showcases cultural heritage of different countries in the region through the stories and cultural items that students share.

Join us for stories of cultural heritage about Iraq. You are welcome to bring items and share your stories if you have a connection to Iraq or simply enjoy learning about Iraqi cultural heritage and the significance of these various items for the students presenting.

posts.caption_raw:
"Join us for a film screening and discussion 

*Foragers*

The film depicts the dramas around the practice of foraging for wild edible plants in Palestine/Israel with wry humor and a meditative pace. Shot in the Golan Heights, the Galilee and Jerusalem, it employs fiction, documentary and archival footage to portray the impact of Israeli nature protection laws on these customs. The restrictions prohibit the collection of the artichoke-like ’akkoub and za’atar (thyme), and have resulted in fines and trials for hundreds caught collecting these native plants. For Palestinians, these laws constitute an ecological veil for legislation that further alienates them from their land while Israeli state representatives insist on their scientific expertise and duty to protect. Following the plants from the wild to the kitchen, from the chases between the foragers and the nature patrol, to courtroom defenses, Foragers captures the joy and knowledge embodied in these traditions alongside their resilience to the prohibitive law. By reframing the terms and constraints of preservation, the film raises questions around the politics of extinction, namely who determines what is made extinct and what gets to live on.

For access and questions, message us here or email us at gmes@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join us for a Film Screening and Discussion 🎥

📽A Summer Not to Forget صيف لا ينسى

A documentary film by Carol Mansour about Israel's war on Lebanon in 2006 presents footage not shown on Western media.

“A Summer Not to Forget” takes you beyond the news headlines into the harsh realities of war. On July 12th, 2006, in retaliation for the capture by Hizbullah of two Israeli soldiers near the border, Israel unleashed wrath on Lebanon for 34 days, subjecting large parts of the country to continuous bombardment. It presents footage that was never shown by Western media. It exposes the devastation and horror of 57 massacres, through the eyes of witnesses and survivors and their families.". ‎

posts.caption_raw:
"Join us for a teach-in and reflection space about Palestine as a Reproductive Justice issue. From Georgia, to Sudan, to Falasteen, we cannot trust any state entity to center our reproductive well-being. HEART will share findings from their latest campaign, RAHIM, on what Islam as a faith can offer to reground in communal care and a global commitment to reproductive agency.

In collaboration with HEART, Students for Justice in Palestine at UIC, the Women's Leadership and Resource Center, and the Arab American Cultural Center.

Please register below to receive more information including location of event.

https://secure.qgiv.com/for/rjfund/event/heartoncampus/".

posts.caption_raw:
"📣 Call for Submissions; Fragments of Ourselves

The Arab American Cultural Center invites you to join our art showcase exploring the intricacies of identity. How has your identity shaped you? What significance does identity hold for you?

What We’re Looking For:

💫 Artwork that reflects the theme of Identity. This can be interpreted in a variety of ways, from explorations of personal identity to reflecting on the experience of being part of a larger community/diaspora.

🖨 Submissions that span across diverse art forms. We welcome paintings, photography, sculptures, spoken word pieces, mixed media installations, and more.

🖌 Art that uplifts and empowers oppressed communities. This showcase is an opportunity to platform voices that have been silenced and to celebrate the beauty of our identities and cultures.

Submission Guidelines:

🌟 The open call is open to artists of all backgrounds and experience levels.

🌟UIC student/alumni

🌟 Be able to provide actual/copies of work.

🌟 Submissions must be submitted online by October 31st, 2024.

🌟 There is no fee to submit.

🌟 Selected artwork will be exhibited at the Arab American Center at UIC throughout the Fall Semester.

Link in the Linktree bio".

posts.caption_raw:
"This Thursday October 10th at 4p.m

Arab and Muslim DEI on College Campuses: Repairing the Ruptures

with Dr, Evelyn Alsultany

"Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion" initiatives have created opportunities of Middle Eastern and North African (MENA) students and Muslim students. Yet such efforts can be rendered meaningless when these same students are targeted for their ace, religion, or expression of free speech on college campuses. In this talk, Dr. Evelyn Alsutlany will review examples of DEI initiatives that have created more inclusive campuses for MENA and Muslim students. She will also review how such initiates fail when it comes to the politics of Israel and Palestine on college campuses. Central to this discussion will be the role of crises-such as Donald Trump's "Muslim ban" or the ongoing war in Israel and Gaza-in opening or closing opportunities for inclusion. How are diversity initiatives enhanced and foreclosed by crisis moments? How can university administrators create and maintain inclusive campus environments through politicized crises?

Dr. Alsutlany is Professor of American Studies and Ethnicity at the University of Southern California's Dornsife College and the author of Broken: the Failed Promise of Muslim Inclusion (NYU Press, 2022), one of the Chronicle of Higher Education's 10 best scholarly books of 2022.".

posts.caption_raw:
"Congratulations to our very own Dr. Nadine Naber @thesaltyfeminista having been selected to join the latest group of Freedom Scholars, a distinction from the Marguerite Casey Foundation that recognizes “exceptional scholar-activists committed to advancing justice and equity.”

 For two decades, Naber, an author, public speaker and activist, has researched and done community work involving racial justice, gender justice, women of color feminisms, Arab and Muslim feminisms, Arab Americans and Muslim Americans. At UIC, she is a professor of gender and women’s studies, Global Asian studies and anthropology. 

Through the Chicago-based organization MAMAS and the United Nations Special Rapporteurs on Racial Discrimination and Torture, she currently helps lead research on activism by people who are mothering in the face of police violence, militarism and migration restrictions. 

“This work supports impacted mothers of color participating in feminist, decolonial and abolitionist policy processes and freedom movements and will culminate in a book,” Naber said.".

posts.caption_raw:
"Disability Justice in Practice: Responding to Palestine
Tuesday, October 8th, 4:00-5:30pm

On Zoom
Register to receive the Zoom information at https://go.uic.edu/DJinPractice (link in bio)

Co-sponsored by the UIC Arab American Cultural Center

Since its inception, disability justice movements have been clear about Palestinian liberation as a disability justice issue, pointing to the interconnections between disablement and ableism, racism, and settler-colonial tactics (for example, check Sins Invalid's 2014 video [in our bio]).). It’s also lifted up a politic of radical love that centers self-determination and cross-movement solidarity.

This event brings together three Chicago-area disabled activists to share how they have negotiated putting disability justice into practice through their response to the ongoing genocide: a writer and media critic, a fashion designer, and a scholar/spoken word poet. Join us!

IDs: Both graphics have a light gray keffiyeh pattern with red poppies along the bottom, with a Disability Cultural Center and Arab American Cultural Center logo at the top.
ID 1: Bold black text says "Disability Justice in Practice: Responding to Palestine, Tues, Oct. 8, 4:00-5:30pm, On Zoom, https://go.uic.edu/DJinPractice"
ID 2: icons for ASL and captions with "Access info: CART and ASL will be provided. Contact dcc@uic.edu with any other access requests!" and a blurb (transcribed above).

@arabamcc @uicdiversity @uic.dcc".

posts.caption_raw:
"🎵Join the Arab American Cultural Center for a Conversation and Performance with Palestinian American artist Clarissa Bitar on Resistance through Music.

Clarissa Bitar is an accomplished Palestinian oud musician and composer hailing from Los Angeles, California. With a background in Music and a specialization in Ethnomusicology from UC Santa Barbara, Clarissa's musical journey has been enriched by training under esteemed Arab oudists such as Simon Shaheen, Charbel Rouhana, and Bassam Saba. Their performances have graced stages across the United States and internationally. Innovative in their approach, Clarissa seamlessly blends the traditional sound of the oud with various genres including R&B, Pop, Hip Hop, and Rap. Their latest album 'Hassan Sabi', showcases their versatility and creativity, available for streaming on all major platforms. Clarissa's music has garnered attention across diverse mediums including Netflix, radio, film, and international exhibits, reflecting the universal appeal and impact of their artistry. Noteworthy releases include the collaborative poetry-oud EP with Palestinian poet Mohammed El-Kurd, titled 'Bellydancing on Wounds', and their solo EP 'Bayati', both demonstrating Clarissa's commitment to exploring new dimensions in music and storytelling.

Clarissa has a passion for teaching and sharing knowledge on Arab music, as well as performing and hopes to continue to carve out spaces for oud and share this tradition with others.

Refreshments will be Served.

Space is wheelchair accessible.

Contact
Arab American Cultural Center 312-413-3253 arabamcc@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw:
"Combating Hate Crimes: A Presentation and Discussion with Assistant State's Attorney, Wesam

The presentation will address what is a hate crime according to Illinois laws and how are these crimes/incidents addressed legally and socially.

Wesam Shahed is a first-generation Palestinian-American from Chicago, Illinois. He serves as a Policy Advisor - Assistant State’s Attorney at the Cook County State’s Attorney’s Office (CCSAO), the second-largest prosecutor’s office in the US. Wesam has not only been recognized as one of the most promising young talents in the CCSAO, but he has also been internationally recognized in the Arab American Foundation’s “30 Under 30.” Wesam recently gave a TEDx Talk on criminal justice reform titled "Does Justice Always Require a Conviction?" Wesam’s involvement ranges far beyond his day job, as he is the Finance Director for the first Muslim and Palestinian American to be elected by the Democratic Party into Illinois’s House Legislature in 2023. Wesam holds executive leadership positions for the Arab Professional Business Association (APBA), the Arab American Bar Association, the Muslim Bar Association, the AB Korkor Mental Health Foundation, and the ACLU Illinois.

 September 10, 2024

12:00 PM - 1:00 PM".

posts.caption_raw:
"📣 Attention Students

We will be hosting our Student Leadership Institute on Friday August 30th - September 1, 2024. The Arab American Cultural Center’s Leadership Institute aims to equip students to enact change at their institutions and in their communities. 

Students train with ArabAmCC staff, UIC partners, UIC alumni, and MENA-led community organizations to gain the tools and literacy to advocate for themselves & their communities, in & beyond the classroom.

The deadline for application is July 12th, 2024. PLEASE NOTE YOU MUST ATTEND ALL THREE DAYS OF THE INSTITUTE.".

posts.caption_raw:
"Save the Date! 
Sunday April 28th, 2024

Join the Arab American Cultural Center in celebrating our graduates of 2024. Enjoy an evening filled with celebrations of the accomplishments of our students, honoring their efforts and those of their families, listening to words of wisdom, and enjoying cultural performances.

Keynote Address: Dr. Thaer Ahmad
Thaer Ahmad, MD is a board certified emergency medicine physician at Advocate Christ Medical Center. Thaer also serves as the Assistant Program Director for the Emergency Medicine Residency Program at Advocate Christ and the Global Health Director for the emergency department at Advocate Christ. He is also an Assistant Clinical Professor at the University of Illinois at Chicago. And a board member of MedGlobal a medical humanitarian NGO that works in Gaza. Thaer serves as the medical ethics director for the emergency department at Advocate Christ. He has been to Gaza/Palestine, Syria, Jordan, Turkey, Kenya, and Greece as a part of projects that focus on strengthening local healthcare capacity.

Student Speaker, Spoken Word Performances by UIC and High School Students, and Music

Space is accessible by wheelchair.

For questions and info, arabamcc@uic.edu

Parking is available in the Wood Parking Structure (1100 Wood St.)".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join the Arab American Cultural Center and the Global Middle East Studies Working Group in a book talk and discussion with Mejdulene Bernard Shomali of her book Between Banat: Queer Arab Critique and Transnational Arab Archives.

“Mejdulene Bernard Shomali’s Between Banat will shatter everything you ever imagined you wanted out of a queer archive. Rejecting hetero-Orientalist binaries, Between Banat creates an epistemology of ‘between’—a generative way of being, knowing, and desiring that constantly moves toward joyful freedom by sidestepping demands for legibility and authenticity. Theorizing intimacy outside of hetero- and homonormative frameworks, Between Banat is a long-awaited, lyrical love letter that invites us to forge collective, liberatory, queer Arab futures.” — Amira Jarmakani, author of An Imperialist Love Story: Desert Romances and the War on Terror

Event is co-sponsored by The Gender and Women's Studies Department, the Gender and Sexuality Center and the Institute for the Humanities

Space is accessible by wheelchair. For other questions, email us at arabamcc@uic.edu

Contact
Arab American Cultural Center (312) 413-3253 arabamcc@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join the Global Middle East Studies Working Group and the Arab American Cultural Center in a book talk and discussion by Prof. Nouri Gana about his latest book, Melancholy Acts: Defeat and Cultural Critique in the Arab World.

Nouri Gana is Professor of Comparative Literature and Near Eastern Languages and Cultures at the University of California, Los Angeles. He is the author of Signifying Loss: Toward a Poetics of Narrative Mourning (2011) and editor of The Making of the Tunisian Revolution: Contexts, Architects, Prospects (2013) and The Edinburgh Companion to the Arab Novel in English: The Politics of Anglo Arab and Arab American Literature and Culture (2013).".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join the Arab American Cultural Center in celebrations of Eid and Eid food items from across the region. Eid in Arabic means festival or holiday.

April this year holds Eid El-Fitr عيد الفطر at the end of Ramadan and preparations for Eid Al-Fiseh (Eastern Easter) عيد الفصح and sham e-nassem شم النسيم which will take place early May.

Families across the region spend Eid with each other being grateful for good health and enjoying good food and each other's company.

This event is part of Arab American Heritage month events. The Arab American Cultural Center is organizing a series of events to celebrate Arab American Heritage Month during April. The state of Illinois designated April as Arab American Heritage Month (AAHM) in 2018 and the Department of State recognized it nationally in 2021. 

During April, Arabs and Arab Americans are honored and celebrated for their historical contributions, culture, traditions, history, arts, and overall social presence as a community in the United States.". ‎

posts.caption_raw:
"Let's Go Class of 2024!!! 🎓

The ArabAmCC will be hosting the Arab American Annual Graduation Haflah for graduating students, families, and friends.

There is NO LIMIT To how many people you can invite to your graduation. Bring your Whole family, village, community...

If you are graduating Spring 2024, Summer or Fall 2024, please fill this form MONDAY April 1, at 11:59 P.M and join us for our annual commencement ceremony! 

Early registration is preferred.
The Graduation Haflah will take place on Sunday April 28th, 2024 at Student Center West at 4p.m.
PLEASE NOTE: You DO NOT have to be Arab to participate in the ArabAmCC graduation. All are welcome

If you have any questions about this form or the Arab American Cultural Center's commencement ceremony, please email arabamcc@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw:
"In the face of ongoing genocide and wars in Palestine, the Arab American Cultural Center at UIC invites you to our Rooted in Resilience Art Showcase and Launch. 

🎨 An art showcase that celebrates the strength and creativity of our communities. We believe that art has the power to heal, inspire, and create change. “Rooted in Resilience” is an opportunity to celebrate our shared humanity and to envision a future where justice and equality prevail.

This showcase exclusively features the work of UIC Students and Alumni; come meet the artists on Feb. 29, 12-2pm, and check out their work! All art will be displayed for the remainder of the semester.

Contact
Arab American Cultural Center (312) 413-3253 arabamcc@uic.edu".

posts.caption_raw:
"We invite Palestinian and Arab American women and nonbinary students at UIC to join us for an evening of tatreez and conversation, healing, and community.

No experience necessary! Supplies will be provided.

Tatreez instruction provided by Myasar Ihmud, Palestinian-American attorney and UIC Law alumnus. Conversation facilitated by Dr. Sarah Abboud, Assistant Professor, Human Development Nursing Science.

Co-sponsored by the Arab American Cultural Center, Students for Justice in Palestine, and College of Medicine Middle Eastern and North African Student Association. Thank you to the Odehmenan Center for hosting us in their lovely new space!
Food, Safety, and Accessibility:

Masks are required.

Dinner will be provided.

Registration link: https://wlrc.uic.edu/events/stitching-resistance/

Please contact us with any questions or access requests: wlrc@uic.edu or (312) 413-1025.".

UIC’s very own Black Student Union 🥳

posts.caption_raw:
"The UIC Black Cultural Center knows that the start of new school year can be a hectic time for everyone, so here are a few things we think will help with the transition from summer to school:

1) Alarms: 
- set them to wake up, have a snack break, time your studying, take a nap, etc.— alarms are a helpful way to make sure you complete things in a timely manner 

2) Notebooks & Writing Utensils: 
- every class might not require one and you might not be a pen-to-paper kind of note-taker, but having a physical notebook and some pens to write with can be super helpful!

3) Laptop and/or Tablet
- most, if not all, classes require a reliable device to access class platforms like Blackboard, Perusall, Moodle, etc.so make sure you find/have a laptop, tablet, or dependable device! 
(visit satech.uic.edu, it.uic.edu, or the UIC library to learn more about alternative access to technology equipment)

4) SNACKS!
- it’s easy to get lost in the hustle and bustle of the day and even week, but you HAVE to eat AND stay hydrated— so if you’re on the go, grab a fruit or one of those adult “lunchables”, make a sandwich, keep some snack bars, a water bottle (reusable is highly recommended 🌎❗️), and make sure you eat!
(visit wellnesscenter.uic.edu to learn more about resources and services regarding food support)

5) Class GroupMe and group chats:
- we get it; talking to people– new or not– can be awkward and intimidating, but joining class chats (no matter the platform) can help you stay informed about what’s going on in class and connect with peers that might be experiencing the same things as you

6) Agenda Tracker
- the class schedule, events happening around campus, birthdays, assignments, and more…it’s all gotta go somewhere! From gCal to a physical planner or reminder sticky notes around your room– find what works for you to keep track of what you have going on in your life

If you ever need a place to help you connect with or locate resources, or even somewhere to destress and hang out, we invite you to come see us at THE Black Cultural Center! 🎉".

posts.caption_raw:
"Welcome Back to School Flames! 🔥📚

The UIC Black Cultural Center is thrilled to welcome you back to campus for what we hope will be a successful and fulfilling school year 🎉

We invite you to join us at our events and programs throughout the year, helping us to cultivate community by taking the time and opportunity to enrich one another so that we might grow together. 🪴

Be on the lookout for upcoming events happening very soon and have an amazing year! 🥳

#theBCCatUlCishere

#CelebrateBlackJoy 
#PromoteBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_raw:
"🪑 Special Edition Black Table Talk 
UIC BCC x DCC: Reading Discussion of Sami Schalk’s “𝑩𝒍𝒂𝒄𝒌 𝑫𝒊𝒔𝒂𝒃𝒊𝒍𝒊𝒕𝒚 𝑷𝒐𝒍𝒊𝒕𝒊𝒄𝒔”

Join us Wednesday, April 17th, 2024 from 12pm to 1pm in the BCC Gallery and on Zoom 
( https://go.uic.edu/DCCZoom )
to discuss excerpts from Dr. Schalk’s
𝑩𝒍𝒂𝒄𝒌 𝑫𝒊𝒔𝒂𝒃𝒊𝒍𝒊𝒕𝒚 𝑷𝒐𝒍𝒊𝒕𝒊𝒄𝒔, the connections it draws, and the questions it provokes. 

⭐️ Book Giveaway of Dr. Schalk’s, 𝑩𝒍𝒂𝒄𝒌 𝑫𝒊𝒔𝒂𝒃𝒊𝒍𝒊𝒕𝒚 𝑷𝒐𝒍𝒊𝒕𝒊𝒄𝒔 while supplies generously sponsored by ACCESS LIVING Arts & Culture Project last!

⭐️ Lunch will be provided! 
(we will have boxed/take-away friendly vegan and gluten-free options)

This event is hosted by UIC’s Black Cultural Center, Disability Cultural Center, and the Provost Initiative on the Racialized Body. 
Co-sponsors:
- ACCESS LIVING Arts & Culture Project 
- UIC Department of Black Studies

🚨⚠️COVID SAFETY⚠️🚨
More masking means more safety for immunocompromised people in our communities. Because lunch will be served, people will likely be unmasked to eat and drink. Please wear a mask when you’re not actively eating or drinking! We will have extras on hand.

ACCESS INFORMATION: 
We will have CART (live captions) in the Zoom and projected into the space. 
The BCC gallery is on the 2nd floor, and the elevator is located in the adjacent, connected building, Burnham Hall.

Flyer 1 ID: On a fuscia background a flier features the text above and an image in the bottom right of the book, “Black Disability Politics.”
Flier 2 ID: On a fuscia background a flier features the text above and the logos of the co-sponsors mentioned above.".

posts.caption_raw:
"🌱🌱🌱UIC LCC x BCC x EARTH MONTH 🌱🌱🌱

In collaboration with the @bccatuic and @sustainableuic Join us for a Hazel Johnson Collage Workshop and Podcast Party on April 18th!

This event is our first ARTivism in honor of Earth Month! Join us April 18th from 1pm-3pm at the UIC Latino Cultural Center while we honor the legacy of the mother of environmental justice, Hazel Johnson.

We will be listening to Respiar Media’s podcast, Help This Garden Grow, which tells the story of Hazel Johnson, a resident of the far south side neighborhood Altgeld Gardens and founder of @pcrchi. We will create collages for environmental justice as we listen to the first two episodes of the podcast and learn from a multi-generational conversation between organizers, policymakers and land stewards. 

We will be joined by community organizers from People for Community Recovery and the Southeast Environmental Task Force! 🤝

Keep an eye out for our other Earth Month programs and we hope to see you there!

All events are free and open to the public! The LCC is wheelchair accessible! 

🌸🌸🌸
*Help This Garden Grow image from Respiar Media’s podcast Cover*".

posts.caption_raw:
"💚🧠 It gets rough this time in the semester, so we invite you to check in and join us tomorrow, Friday April 5th from 10am-11am for our monthly initiative, “What’s Going On?” 

“What’s Going On?” is a monthly event with presentations and discussions on various mental health topics. Students have the opportunity to share their experiences on a range of topics focusing specifically on Black Mental Health and Wellness. The topic for April is 
Self Love Through Self-Care ❤️‍🩹

For more details:
go.uic.edu/whats-going-on

Questions? Email Dr. Belva Miles at belva1@uic.edu or blacc@uic.edu

#BCCWhatsGoingOn #talkaboutmentalhealth #blackmentalhealth #mentalhealthmatters #selfloveselfcare 
#UICcounselingcenter
#BCCatUIC
#theBCCatUICishere
#wearetheBCCatUIC #UlCblackculturalcenter
#blackstudentengagementatUIC #uicblackstudentbody
#CelebrateBlackJoy
#PromoteBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_raw:
"🌟 Late Nites at the BCC will be this Thursday, April 4th, 2024 from 5pm - 9pm !!!

If you didn’t know, we (the BCC) are opening our space for longer hours so students have a safe, welcoming space to study, relax, and hangout.
Our gallery space will be for those who want a more lax environment to study and cultivate community in. Our library/lounge space across the hall will be for those who would like a more quiet space to focus.

Bring your homework, some snacks, and maybe a friend or two (or several 👀) and get your study on! 📚

Seats are first come first serve, so be there and don’t forget to check-in using the QR codes that will be posted on the doors!

#latenightsattheBCC
#BCCatUIC
#theBCCatUICishere
#wearetheBCCatUIC #UlCblackculturalcenter
#blackstudentengagementatUIC #uicblackstudentbody
#CelebrateBlackJoy
#PromoteBlackExcellence".

We have heard you and are excited to say that
THIS EVENT WILL BE HELD IN A LARGER SPACE!

Bring your poems, songs, acts, and friends to the SCE CARDINAL ROOM for a night of arts and expression!

Doors open at 6pm! Don’t miss it!

*this event is open to ALL and performance signups are accepted before and during the event to our female and femme-identifying community members ONLY if you aren’t already listed to perform

RSVP/sign up using this link ⬇️:

https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSddJRrDT6kTh11ykiLrc_n-QtKq-7qxowjJ2q2g1D3fFccAmA/viewform

#celebratewomen
#celebrateBlackwomen
#supportBlackcreatives
#supportBlackart
#supportBlackstudents
#BCCopenmicnight
#wearetheBCCatUIC 
#UICblackculturalcenter
#blackstudentengagementatUIC
#uicblackstudentbody 
#CelebratingBlackJoy
#PromotingBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_raw:
"🌟 Late Nites at the BCC will be this Thursday, March 7th, 2024 from 5pm - 9pm !!!

If you didn’t know, we (the BCC) are opening our space for longer hours so students have a safe, welcoming space to study, relax, and hangout.
Our gallery space will be for those who want a more lax environment to study and cultivate community in. Our library/lounge space across the hall will be for those who would like a more quiet space to focus.

Bring your homework, some snacks, and maybe a friend or two (or several 👀) and get your study on! 📚

Seats are first come first serve, so be there and don’t forget to check-in using the QR codes that will be posted on the doors! 

#latenightsattheBCC
#BCCatUIC
#theBCCatUICishere
#wearetheBCCatUIC #UICblackculturalcenter
#blackstudentengagementatUIC #uicblackstudentbody
#CelebrateBlackJoy
#PromoteBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_raw:
"🧘🏾 Are you looking for a quick break amidst a busy day? Packed schedule and midterm season got you feeling all over the place? Was rest and relaxation on your New Year’s Resolution list for 2024? 

Come to the Black Cultural Center this Wednesday, March 6th from 12pm-1pm for R&B: Rest and Balance! 💆🏾✨

While the beautiful sounds of R&B play in the background, we will be reflecting and visualizing how you take care of yourself this year, from allocating time for new hobbies to squashing bad habits. Enjoy snacks, community, and tea while making Pinterest boards, creating routines, putting together a new playlist, and much more! 🪴

Bring a friend and join us as we reclaim and love our minds, bodies, and selves 😌💚

*this event is open to ALL".

"🔊What do you know about some House Music? 

Well no matter what you do or do not know, we invite you to joyously celebrate 40 Years of Chicago House Music and end the month with the Black Cultural Center next Thursday, February 29th from 6pm-9pm! 🎉

Bring your friends and join in the festivities, because the month might be ending but the celebration doesn’t stop 🥳

posts.caption_raw:
"🌟 This month is a Black History Month Special “What’s Going On?” where we will be coloring, creating collages/vision boards, and making bracelets as a way to reduce stress and gather together to cultivate and celebrate creativity. 
❤️💛💚🖤

“What’s Going On?” is a monthly event with presentations and discussions on various mental health topics. Students have the opportunity to share their experiences on a range of topics focusing specifically on Black Mental Health and Wellness. 

Join us at the Black Cultural Center (830 S. Halsted St, Addams Hall, Room 209) next Friday, February 9th, 2024 from 10am - 12pm!

***Art supplies and refreshments will be provided so be sure to RSVP via our website!

Questions? Email Dr. Belva Miles at belva1@uic.edu or blacc@uic.edu

#BCCWhatsGoingOn
#BlackHeritageMonth2024
#BHM2024
#BCCatUIC
#theBCCatUICishere
#wearetheBCCatUIC #UICblackculturalcenter
#blackstudentengagementatUIC #uicblackstudentbody
#CelebrateBlackJoy
#PromoteBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_clean:
"🌟Our first Late Nites at the BCC will be next Thursday, February 8th, 2024 from 5pm - 8pm 🥳

If you didn’t know, we (the BCC) are opening our space for longer hours so students have a safe, welcoming space to study, relax, and hangout

Our gallery space will be for those who want a more lax environment to study and cultivate community in. Our library/lounge space across the hall will be for those who would like a more quiet space to focus.

Bring your homework, some snacks, and maybe a friend or two (or several 👀) and get your study on! 📚

Seats are first come first serve, so be there and don’t forget to check-in using the QR codes that will be posted on the doors! 

#latenightsattheBCC
#BlackHeritageMonth2024
#BHM2024
#BCCatUIC
#theBCCatUICishere
#wearetheBCCatUIC #UICblackculturalcenter
#blackstudentengagementatUIC #uicblackstudentbody
#CelebrateBlackJoy
#PromoteBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_raw:
"👟🎶 Explore the history, politics, and Afro-Diasporic spiritual roots of Chicago footwork with the Black Cultural Center next Wednesday, February 7th in our two-part Footwork event! 

Learn how footwork is an embodied and creative form of resistance to state-sanctioned violence and environmental racism

Please email us at blacc@uic.edu with any questions.

We look forward to seeing you!

posts.caption_raw:
"🪑Happy Friday Flames! 🌟🔥
What does it mean to have a goal? How do you think your year will go? How do you want it to go? Are there things you’d like to accomplish in the next few months? Any big plans for the summer? 
🤔

Channel your creativity and figure out if those questions have answers when you join us in creating vision boards and setting goals for 2024 this upcoming Wednesday, January 31st at our first Black Table Talk of the semester! 

(supplies will be provided)

See you there!

#BCCatUICBlackTableTalk
#BCCatUIC
#theBCCatUICishere
#wearetheBCCatUIC #UlCblackculturalcenter
#blackstudentengagementatUIC #uicblackstudentbody
#CelebrateBlackJoy
#PromoteBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_raw:
"📚 ‘Tis the season of long nights, heavy eyes, and lots of stress

Alleviate some of those finals season symptoms by coming to the BCC next week from December 4th-7th to utilize our space for studying and decompressing 💆

We will have hot cocoa, tea, and treats for you to enjoy while you work, and if you feel the need to take a brain break, we will also have supplies for you to create ornaments and crafts which we will be donating to the children of UI Health 🎄

Bring your study materials, assignments, favorite snacks, some friends, etc. to the BCC and let’s get through finals season together 🤞".

We look forward to seeing you!

posts.caption_raw:
"☕️ Warm up and unwind with the BCC next Monday, November 6th, 2023 at our first ever Hot Cocoa Social! 

This event is open to ALL, so join us, bring some friends, and any games you might have for a night of fun and community 🤝

The Omicron Beta Chapter of Sigma Gamma Rho Sorority, Inc. will be taking preorders for their Insomnia Cookie Fundraiser at this event, so be on the lookout for their members to place your orders! (more details @sgrho_ob )
 
*hot cocoa will be first come first serve, so don’t be late!".

posts.caption_raw:
"The statement from the Social Justice Scholars on the Humanitarian Crisis in Gaza is supported by the Black Cultural Center. 

We at the Black Cultural Center feel it imperative to acknowledge the ongoing tragedies and violence mounting in israel-occupied Palestine. We mourn and stand with the communities affected both abroad and within the U.S., even more recently in our very own city.
The Black Cultural Center will continue to be a welcoming and safe space for our UIC community.".

posts.caption_raw:
"🌟 Meet us at the BCC next Thursday for our second “Late Nights at the BCC” 

We had such a great time laughing and studying with you all last week so we want to keep that going! 
If you didn’t know, we (the BCC) are opening our space for longer hours so students have a safe, welcoming space to study, relax, and hangout 🤓 

BTW: Our gallery space will be where those who want a more lax environment to study and community in can go. Our library/lounge space across the hall will be for those who would like a more quiet space to focus 🤗

Bring your homework, some snacks, and maybe a friend or two (or several 👀) and get your study on! 📚

Seats are first come first serve, so be there and don’t forget to check-in using the QR codes that will be posted on the doors! 🎉

#theBCC #latenightsattheBCC #UICBlackCulturalCenter #wearehere".

posts.caption_raw:
"🪑Join us at our next Black Table Talk as we tackle the fallacies, myths, and hearsay of this great— or not so great 👀— city we reside in! 

Whether you’re a newcomer, season-by-season resident, or Chicago native, pull up a chair with us this Wednesday, September 27th from 12pm-1pm!

We look forward to seeing you! 

#BCCatUICBlackTableTalk

posts.caption_raw:
"Join us as we kick off our annual Black Student Leadership Conference in partnership with the Black Cultural Center! It's a gathering of visionary student leaders, pooling their ideas to enhance the Black student experience at UIC. 🫱🏽‍🫲🏾 
While not everyone can physically attend, your voice matters! 💬 Share your insights in the weeks to come, ensuring your ideas shape the conversation! Let's make change together. 📢💪🏾
If you are a student org. leader, the link to register is in our bio! #CelebratingBlackJoy #BSOdayatUIC2023  #PromoteBlackExcellence".

posts.caption_raw:
"Meet us at the BCC this Thursday for our very first “Late Nights at the BCC” 🌟
We are opening our space for longer hours so students have a safe, welcoming space to study, relax, and hangout 🤓 
Bring your homework, some snacks, and maybe a friend or two (or several 👀) and get your study on! 📚

Seats are first come first serve, so be there!

#theBCC #latenightsattheBCC #UICBlackCulturalCenter #wearehere".

posts.caption_raw:
"Asked and Received✅ 
UIC NPHC Presents NPHC•𝒊𝒔𝒉
a weeks worth of events hosted by the Divine 9
•
•
The week will be starting off with the Back to Black Movie Night🎥. Be sure to keep a look out for the ig story to vote for which movie you want to see. On Tuesday, join us for Project Think Green to help clean up the UIC neighborhood 🧽 . On Wednesday, pick out your favorite song and grab a partner because it’s Trap Karaoke night🎙🎵. Starting October 7th we will be collecting donations to make blessing bags for homeless, we will be packaging and distributing those items on Thursday in the UIC Area. DM the page or any members of NPHC and they will give you further information on how to donate. And we will be closing out the week with a dinner with D9 and some delicious soul food🍽, RSVP required (link in the bio). We can’t wait to see you all☺️ 💛🦍💗🐸❤️🐰💜🐶❤️🐘💙🕊💙🕊💛🐩😂🐎".

📸 Take the largest UIC logo photo
🖊 Sign the class banner
👕 Rock your red Orientation tee

🍽 Free food too!

Let’s make some unforgettable memories—this is YOUR official start as a UIC Flame! ❤️🔥
#UIC #ThisIsUIC #UICOrientation #UIC2029 #NewFlameWhoDis #FlameOn #UICConvocation #GoFlames #UICProud".

posts.caption_raw:
"❄️ Winter Break Hours Alert! ❄️ (Snowflake emoji)

As the semester winds down, we’re adjusting our hours for the winter break. Please see below for our updated schedule:

📅 [Dec 13-Jan 3]- DCC is closed
[Jan 6-Jan 10] -DCC virtually available 
[Jan 13th]- Back for Spring semester. 

We wish you a warm, safe, and restful winter break! Whether you’re traveling, relaxing, or celebrating, take care and recharge. We’ll see you in the new year! 💙

ID 1: A light blue background with snow hills and trees. The Disability Cultural Center logo is at the top. The words “Winter Break Schedule” is at the top and the winter break hours transcribed above are below this. A sleeping polar bear is in the lower right corner.

ID 2: In the same background the Disability Cultural Center logo is at the top. The words, “Wishing you a warm and restful winter break“ with a sleeping polar bear in the lower right corner.".

posts.caption_raw:
"All My Pretty Fictions with Terri Lynne Hudson

When: Tomorrow, Thursday December 12 at 3:00 pm - 4:30pm CST

Where: UIC Disability, Health and Social Policy Building, 1640 W. Roosevelt Road; Chicago, IL 60608

Terri Lynne Hudson will be doing a multimedia solo performance (with asynchronous collaboration) exploring isolation, the nondisabled gaze on disabled life, and revealing the internal life, imagination and creativity that can happen both when accommodations are offered and when they are denied and disabled people have to carve out their own worlds apart from general public life.

ACCESS INFO: 

The building is fully accessible including ramps, elevators, and a drop off circle if you are being dropped off. Masks are required for this event so that we might be in disability community together. We are strongly encouraging a scent free environment. We will have access doulas at this event to assist you if needed. Please be sure to let us know when registering if you'll need CART and/or ASL services.

Register and read the full event description here: 

https://ahs.uic.edu/events/all-my-pretty-fictions-with-terri-lynne-hudson/. 

ID: This promotional poster features the name of the event, "All My Pretty Fictions with Terri Lynne Hudson," and a drawing of an open book against a white background. Below are three glowing spots with the date, location and registration information. The full event description and logos for co-sponsors are on the bottom of the flier.".

posts.caption_raw:
"Good luck with finals! 📚(Books emoji)

Need a spot to study, recharge, or decompress? The DCC Lounge and Quiet Space are here for you this week!

🗓️ Open Monday through Thursday
🕒 [10 am-4:30 pm]

Take care of yourself, stay hydrated, and remember—you’ve got this! 

Image description: Green background with the disability cultural center logo at the top left corner. An animated picture of the disability cultural center lounge is in the top left corner (Blue brick wall, lamp, person sitting on a grey bean bag). Next to this image are the words “Good luck with finals!” Need a space to study or decompress? 
The DCC Lounge & the DCC Quiet Space are open Monday through Thursday.“ Below these words is a desk with a yellow mug, laptop, books, and a pencil holder with pens in it.

#FinalsWeek #DisabilityCulturalCenter #StudySupport".

posts.caption_raw:
"We're in the final stretch of the semester! Join the students of ART 190: Socially Engaged Art as they host a Cultural Carnival to highlight the seven cultural centers at UIC.

Enjoy free food, live music, face painting, and more! 🍲🎶🖌️

Cultural Carnival
TODAY, Monday, December 2nd from 11 am-4pm
Student Center East, 3rd Floor, Dearborn AB

Stop by anytime!!

At the event students will have an access doula booth, earplugs, facemasks, and quiet zone to get some studying in too 📚

Questions? Contact jmoor31@uic.edu

Thank you to @uic_cada and the students of ART 190 for organizing this event and lifting up the work of the cultural centers! 🎉

ID: Arms with a variety of skin tones stretch to the center, with "cultural carnival" in an arch of green 70s letters.".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join the DCC for Crip Coffee Break ☕️( Coffee emoji)

This is a chill and comfy space to take a break and meet people!

📆Wednesdays, 3-4pm
📍BSB 235 and Zoom

Join us for our last Crip Coffee break of the semester on Wednesday December 4th at 3pm for our open games, game time!

This is an intentional space for people with disabilities (broadly defined) and people exploring their relationship to disability identity to build community with one another. Read the full details, Covid Safety info, and access info on our event listing! (link in bio) Have an assignment? Please join us for one of our other cultural events! Crip Coffee Break is not compatible with event assignments. Thanks for your understanding, and feel free to contact us with any questions.

ID 1: On a lavender background, there is a DCC logo with "Crip Coffee Break" and a coffee mug in the upper left, and captioning and masking icons in the upper right. In the middle on the left side there is a picture of a red Nintendo DS and a blue Nintendo switch. Underneath this are colorful playing cards. Next to this are the words "Weds December 4 @3pm Game Time Open Games (Nintendo, Cards, etc.)"".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join UIC DCC for the last "voices off" ASL get-together, of the semester. We can use this time to introduce ourselves, share what's going on for us, and dig into some Deaf culture tidbits-all in sign.

📆Tuesday, December 3rd, 4-5pm
📍BSB 231 (DCC Quiet Space), 1007 W. Harrison Street

Voices off
This gathering is a friendly, non-judgmental space for using (and practicing) ASL. Learners are welcome!
Open to students, faculty, staff, and community members.
Check out our event listing for more details including access info and Covid safety info: Events page linked in our bio!
ID 1: "ASL Chat" in big, fun green letters with a purple
"y" hand-shape with an arrow, the sign for "THAT." A black DCC logo is at the top, and the event dates, time, and location are below with "voices off."
ID 2: ASL Chat and "THAT" with explanatory blurb in green text (transcribed above).".

posts.caption_raw:
"🤔 What are UIC students doing for mental health? 
🔮 What are students' dreams for UIC? 

This week, the DCC is hosting an event in collaboration with UIC United Faculty Common Good, UIC Student Affairs, and the UIC Office of Equity & Diversity. We will share the results of the research project, Mental Health Storytelling for Disability Justice to explore how we can think about better supporting mental health on the UIC campus.

⭐ State of Student Mental Health at UIC: Storytelling for Disability Justice
Report Release & Open Mic
📅 Weds Nov 20, 3:30-5pm
📍Latino Cultural Center (Lecture Center B) & On Zoom 

We will be drawing on data collected from interviews and focus groups conducted with UIC students in the Spring and Fall 2024 semesters. Join the report authors Nicole Nguyen and Nico Darcangelo to chat about mental health at UIC!
64% of UIC students report they are "not flourishing" in terms of mental health. Yet, fewer than 4% of students use the Counseling Center. 

Come learn what students wish faculty and administration knew about mental health, what barriers students face, where they find support, and most excitingly, what dreams they have for UIC in terms of mental health.
Following a discussion about the report, we will shift to having an open-mic event that will include art sharing, map making, and other activities - all related to mental health! 

If you are a current UIC student, staff, or faculty and are interested in participating in the open-mic or sharing a piece of artwork, please complete the google form (link in bio!). Filling out the form is not a requirement and you can change your mind, but we'd appreciate knowing who to expect in advance if possible. 

We are excited to have you join us! Full description including access info is on our events page (link in bio)! 

Image Description in comments

@uicdiversity @uicfacultyunion @uiclife".

posts.caption_raw:
"Next week! 
Practitioners of Being and Becoming
Tabletop Puppetry and Disability Culture Workshop 
Nov. 15th, 3-5pm
Location: UIC Disability, Health, and Social Policy Building (register for room details)

Limited spots! Please register at: https://go.uic.edu/PuppetryWorkshop

During this free, two hour workshop facilitated by 3Arts/Bodies of Work Resident Artist, Rachel Singer, we will collaborate in small groups to bring a tabletop puppet to life and explore our senses as we work with objects. 

No theater or performance experience needed and sharing to the whole group is always optional.

Artists, educators, activists and healthcare practitioners specialize in being and becoming, but really, we are all experts in our own being and becoming!

The medical model/individual model of disability has pushed us, as individuals and practitioners, into silos of isolation.  We will apply ideas from the political/relational model to explore a path forward.  My hope is to bring people together from many experiences and practices, forming a nourishing space to invite connection. 

Flyer Image Description:
Poster has a sand colored background with purple and orange decorative shapes and two small photos that show a group of people smiling as they bring a brown paper practice puppet on a tabletop to life, each puppeteer holding a different part of the puppet's body to animate it. Along the bottom are logos for the event co-sponsors.

About Rachel Singer:
She is 3 Arts/Bodies of Work artist-in-residence in theater and disability. She has been a teaching artist in the Chicago Public Schools since 2007 for both dance and puppetry and is currently a certified occupational therapy assistant. Her most recent puppetry and dance showing was with Puppet Lab through the Chicago International Puppet Theater Festival. She is continuing to develop this work in her residency.

@bowchicago @3artschicago accessliving @dcal_saic @practiceofimagination @uicdiversity @uicahs @idhduic".

posts.caption_raw:
"In a couple of weeks, the DCC is hosting an event in collaboration with UIC United Faculty Common Good, UIC Student Affairs, and the UIC Office of Equity & Diversity. We will share the results of the research project, Mental Health Storytelling for Disability Justice to explore how we can think about better supporting mental health on the UIC campus.

State of Student Mental Health at UIC: Storytelling for Disability Justice
Report Release & Open Mic

Weds Nov 20, 3:30-5pm
Latino Cultural Center (Lecture Center B) & On Zoom 

We will be drawing on data collected from interviews and focus groups conducted with UIC students in the Spring and Fall 2024 semesters. Join the report authors Nicole Nguyen and Nico Darcangelo to chat about mental health at UIC!

64% of UIC students report they are "not flourishing" in terms of mental health. Yet, fewer than 4% of students use the Counseling Center. 

Come learn what students wish faculty and administration knew about mental health, what barriers students face, where they find support, and most excitingly, what dreams they have for UIC in terms of mental health.

Following a discussion about the report, we will shift to having an open-mic event that will include art sharing, map making, and other activities - all related to mental health! 

If you are a current UIC student, staff, or faculty and are interested in participating in the open-mic or sharing a piece of artwork, please complete the google form (link in bio!). Filling out the form is not a requirement and you can change your mind, but we'd appreciate knowing who to expect in advance if possible. 

We are excited to have you join us! Full description including access info is on our events page (link in bio)! 

Image Description in comments".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join UIC DCC for a "voices off" ASL get-together. This ASL Chat falls on election day! We'll take some time to check in and process all that we're thinking about (voting & feelings vocab will be shared)! 

Tuesday, October 15, 4-5pm
BSB 231 (DCC Quiet Space), 1007 W. Harrison Street
Voices off
This gathering is a friendly, non-judgmental space for using (and practicing) ASL. Learners are welcome! Open to students, faculty, staff, and community members.
For the rest of Fall 2024, we'll meet on the first and Third Tuesdays of the month (Nov 5 & 19, and Dec 3)
Check out our event listing for more details including access info and Covid safety info: Events page linked in our bio!
ID 1: "ASL Chat" in big, fun green letters with a purple "y" hand-shape with an arrow, the sign for "THAT." A yellow and white ballot image is next to the words “voting edition”. A black DCC logo is at the top, and the event dates, time, and location are below with "voices off."
ID 2: ASL Chat and "THAT" with explanatory blurb in green text (transcribed above).".

posts.caption_raw:
"The Disability Cultural Center Lounges are closing at 1:45pm today (October 31)! We'll be attending an amazing event called "Civic Engagement for Social Justice" If you're free, you should come too! 

Dive deep into the role of civic engagement in social justice and learn how speaking up and taking action can drive meaningful change. Skora, co-founder and Chief Development Officer of @bravespacealliance and creator of the "Girl, I Guess" Progressive Voter Guide, will discuss what it means to engage with current issues and make change on a local level through a progressive lens, from voting, to watchdog research, to protesting against a genocide.

Thursday, October 31, 2024
2 - 4 PM CST
Student Services Building, Conference Room C
1200 W. Harrison St.
Link in @uicwlrc bio to RSVP!

ID 1: Light blue background with the words ”Closing Today” in dark blue font. The date (October 31st) and time (1:45 pm) are below and underneath this are the words, “Swipe to learn about the event we will be attending!”

ID 2: A vibrant event flyer titled "Civic Engagement for Social Justice" at the top, styled in bold white text. Beneath it, the subtitle reads, "Speaking and Acting in Ways That Matter." The background features images of activism: people voting, holding signs, and standing in solidarity. To the right, a circular photo of the speaker, Stephanie Skora, shows them smiling with short hair, glasses, and a tattoo on their hand.".

posts.caption_raw:
"Next week! Come learn about medicinal teas and how the UIC Heritage Garden makes tea! 

✨ Tea Time @ the DCC
with Eyzel Torres-Vicencio and UIC's Heritage Garden

📆 Tuesday Oct 29th at 1pm
📍BSB 235 and on Zoom (https://go.uic.edu/DCCZoom)

In this event, UIC & Heritage Garden alum Eyzel will share her zine, “Plant Power: A Guide to Managing Anxiety Using Medicinal Tea”

Plus, the UIC Heritage Garden will bring their Tea Library, share tea knowledge, and some delicious tea, of course!

Access info: CART (live captions) will be provided & we continue to mask in our spaces. Check out the full event listing (link in bio) for more details and get in touch at dcc@uic.edu! 

ID 1: Across the top are two rows of various tea cups, and below are hexagonal frames, one with a photo of Eyzel, a smiling person with shoulder length wavy black hair, glasses, and dangling earrings, who is standing in front of a mural. The cover of her Plant Power zine is below, with hand lettering and a blue head resting its chin on two hands, with cascading waterfalls for hair. On the right is an image of the Heritage Garden Tea Library featuring a small case with wooden drawers holding tea, with a stack of cups and some tea bags. At the bottom there is a UIC Disability Cultural Center Logo. Text reads Tea Time @ the DCC with basic event details. 
ID 2: Against a similar background, text shares the event blurb (transcribed above) and access info. There are icons for captions and masking. 

@uicdiversity @uicheritagegarden".

posts.caption_raw:
"How can access conflicts inspire artistry? 

Access conflicts (n. pl.) are situations in which one person's access needs clash with another's. 

Tomorrow! Join Terri Lynne Hudson for an interactive talk about her artistic process and how it reimagines access conflict. 

✨Representational Conundrums: The Generative Potential of Access Conflict 
with 2024 3Arts / Bodies of Work Resident Terri Lynne Hudson 

✨Tuesday, October 22, 4:00-5:15pm
On Zoom with a Watch Party at DCC 
https://go.uic.edu/TLHudson

With a watch party at the UIC DCC! 
Behavioral Sciences Building 235
1007 W. Harrison Street 

Join Terri Lynne Hudson, theatre and multi-disciplinary artist, for an interactive talk about her process for the new work, all my pretty fictions. Hudson’s practice explores the generative potential of access conflicts, which are situations in which one's access needs clash with another’s. Rather than presenting insurmountable barriers, these access conflicts can inspire artistry.

Check out our event listing for more information about Hudson as well as access info! (Link in our bio)

ID 1-3: Text about access conflicts (transcribed above) in lavender boxes against a deep blue background with interacting ripples on water.
ID 4: With the same background, event title and artist information in white and lavender boxes. Two black circles have the Date and Time, with a third circle containing a headshot of Hudson, a Black woman with shoulder length hair and a blue shirt, looking intently at the camera. At the bottom of the flyer is a zoom link and underneath the link is the address for the behavioral sciences building, 235 1007 W. Harrison Street. 
ID 5 : A deep blue background with interacting ripples on water, with event description in a white box. Underneath this are logos for 3Arts, Bodies of Work, the National Endowment for the Arts, The Joyce Foundation, The department of Disability and Human Development, and the Disability Cultural Center. On the left side there are CC, ASL and masking symbols.  @bowchicago @uicdiversity @3artschicago".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join the DCC for Crip Coffee Break ( Coffee emoji)
This is a chill and comfy space to take a break and meet people!

Wednesdays, 3-4pm BSB 235 and Zoom
Join us on Wednesday October 23 at 3pm for chess and checkers with Dante!

Upcoming Events:
Oct 30: Get Crafty, Crocheting & Knitting (BYO or learn)
Nov 6: AV Club, DCC Comedy Club (videos)
Nov 13: Game Time, Bingo for a Dreaded Task with Margaret
Nov 20: Get Crafty, Make a DCC Disability Justice Coloring Book
Dec 4: Game Time, Open Games (Nintendo, cards, etc)

This is an intentional space for people with disabilities (broadly defined) and people exploring their relationship to disability identity to build community with one another. Read the full details, Covid Safety info, and access info on our event listing! (link in bio)

Have an assignment? Please join us for one of our other cultural events! Crip Coffee Break is not compatible with event assignments. Thanks for your understanding, and feel free to contact us with any questions.

ID 1: On a lavender background, there is a DCC logo with "Crip Coffee Break" and a coffee mug in the upper left, and captioning and masking icons in the upper right. In the middle on the left side there is a brown chess board with black and beige tiles. Yellow and red checkers pieces are on the bottom. Next to this are the words "Weds October 23 @3pm Game Time Chess and Checkers with Dante. Next up: Crocheting and knitting on 10/30"
ID 2: On a similar background, there's a list of upcoming Crip Coffee Breaks (transcribed in caption)!".

posts.caption_raw:
"Superfest is finally here! Please join the UIC DCC, Bodies of Work, and Chicagoland Disabled People of Color Coalition for the Chicago Showcase tomorrow and Saturday! 

✨ Friday, October 18
4-7pm
Award Winners Screening (Watch Party)

✨ Saturday, October 19
1-3pm
Seven additional films!

On UIC's Campus at Student Center East
Cardinal Room (3rd floor)
750 S. Halsted 
For full details, access info, and Covid safety info, check out the event listings (link in our bio)!

If you can't join us, there are tons of ways to engage with the festival. The Longmore Institute and San Francisco Disability Cultural Center have an amazing line-up of programs! 🩷 (pink heart emoji)

Tonight!! Kickoff Panel with Alice Wong 
Thursday, October 17th 
5:00 - 6:00 PM Central 
Register: dcc.page/superfest24-opening
Access: ASL | CART | virtual

On Sunday there will be a Superfest Community Conversation
Sunday, October 20, 2024
6:00 - 7:30 Central
Register: https://dcc.page/superfest2024-community-chat
Access: ASL | CART | virtual

All weekend long: 
Superfest is now online! Passes are pay what you can, and you can watch all the films online at https://superfest.eventive.org/passes/buy

Image descriptions in the comments".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join UlC DCC for a "voices off" ASL get-together. We can use this time to introduce ourselves, share what's going on for us, and dig into some Deaf culture tidbits-all in sign.
Tuesday, October 15, 4-5pm
BSB 231 (DCC Quiet Space), 1007 W. Harrison Street
Voices off
This gathering is a friendly, non-judgmental space for using (and practicing) ASL. Learners are welcome! Open to students, faculty, staff, and community members.
For the rest of Fall 2024, we'll meet on the first and Third Tuesdays of the month (Oct 15, Nov 5 & 19, and Dec 3)
Check out our event listing for more details including access info and Covid safety info: Events page linked in our bio!
ID 1: "ASL Chat" in big, fun green letters with a purple “y” hand-shape with an arrow, the sign for "THAT." A black DCC logo is at the top, and the event dates, time, and location are below with "voices off."
ID 2: ASL Chat and "THAT" with explanatory blurb in green text (transcribed above).".

posts.caption_raw:
"We are very excited to be holding a zine making workshop at UIC’s Disability Cultural Center! Please register at the link in bio. 

ID: The first slide has a purple background and two black and white images of people posing for a photo and making art. The middle of the screen reads “HarmoniZine” in cursive. Symmetrical black splatters are at the bottom with white text inside that reads “Thursday, 10/17 4:30-7pm Disability Cultural Center 1007 W. Harrison St. Visual and audio zine making on disability harmony. Here and everywhere.
Second page states access info. The DCC is on the second floor and accessible by elevator. The room we’ll be in has adjustable non fluorescent lights and a lamp. Chairs are lightweight and armless with wheels. A medicine ball chair and chairs without wheels both with or without arms are available. There are ground mats and a bed if you need to lie down. Restrooms vary in access. The first floor has a scented, roomy single use restroom with an automatic door. The second floor has a multi- stall all-gender restroom with less space. Masks are required unless it’s an access need or if you’re eating/drinking. We will have multiple available. If you have any questions or concerns please email cuerposjustificados@gmail.com or DM us @ cuerposjustificados. End description.".

"The Chicago Showcase of Superfest will be jampacked with great films--here's the line-up for Saturday, October 19 (1-3pm)! 

🎬 Saturday Films

Dancer, Mongolia/Australia, 2023 (Drama, 13 min)
Mo <3 Kyra, UK, 2023 (Drama, 13 min)
Ten to One, USA, 2023 (Comedy, 6 min)
Tie Your Camel and Trust in God, Canada, 2021 (Documentary, 12 min)

posts.caption_raw:
"Join the DCC for Crip Coffee Break ☕️( Coffee emoji)
This is a chill and comfy space to take a break and meet people!

Wednesdays, 3-4pm BSB 235 and Zoom

Join us on Wednesday October 2nd at 3pm for Game Time! We'll be playing cards and chatting.

Upcoming Events:
Oct 9: Get Crafty, Paint & Sip (Open Paint Studio)
Oct 23: Game Time, Chess & Checkers with Dante
Oct 30: Get Crafty, Crocheting & Knitting (BYO or learn!)
Nov 6: AV Club, DCC Comedy Club (videos)
Nov 13: Game Time, Bingo for a Dreaded Task with Margaret
Nov 20: Get Crafty, Make a DCC Disability Justice Coloring Book
Dec 4: Game Time, Open Games (Nintendo, cards, etc.)

This is an intentional space for people with disabilities (broadly defined) and people exploring their relationship to disability identity to build community with one another. Read the full details, Covid Safety info, and access info on our event listing! (link in bio)

Have an assignment? Please join us for one of our other cultural events! Crip Coffee Break is not compatible with event assignments. Thanks for your understanding, and feel free to contact us with any questions.

Image Description in comments".

posts.caption_raw:
"Our first ASL Chat is this week! Join UIC DCC for a “voices off” ASL get-together. We can use this time to introduce ourselves, share what's going on for us, and dig into some Deaf culture tidbits–all in sign.

Tuesday, October 1, 4-5pm
BSB 231 (DCC Quiet Space), 1007 W. Harrison Street 
Voices off

This gathering is a friendly, non-judgmental space for using (and practicing) ASL. Learners are welcome! Open to students, faculty, staff, and community members. 

For the rest of Fall 2024, we'll meet on the first and Third Tuesdays of the month (Oct 15, Nov 5 & 19, and Dec 3)

Check out our event listing for more details including access info and Covid safety info: Events page linked in our bio! 

ID 1: "ASL Chat" in big, fun green letters with a purple y handshape with an arrow, the sign for "THAT." A black DCC logo is at the top, and the event dates, time, and location are below with "voices off."
ID 2: ASL Chat and "THAT" with explanatory blurb in green text (transcribed above).".

posts.caption_raw:
"Disability Justice in Practice: Responding to Palestine
Tuesday, October 8
4:00-5:30pm
On Zoom
Register to receive the Zoom information at https://go.uic.edu/DJinPractice (link in bio)

Co-sponsored by the UIC Arab American Cultural Center

Since its inception, disability justice movements have been clear about Palestinian liberation as a disability justice issue, pointing to the interconnections between disablement and ableism, racism, and settler-colonial tactics (for example, check Sins Invalid's 2014 video [in our bio]).). It’s also lifted up a politic of radical love that centers self-determination and cross-movement solidarity.

This event brings together three Chicago-area disabled activists to share how they have negotiated putting disability justice into practice through their response to the ongoing genocide: a writer and media critic, a fashion designer, and a scholar/spoken word poet. Join us!

IDs: Both graphics have a light gray keffiyeh pattern with red poppies along the bottom, with a Disability Cultural Center and Arab American Cultural Center logo at the top. 
ID 1: Bold black text says "Disability Justice in Practice: Responding to Palestine, Tues, Oct. 8, 4:00-5:30pm, On Zoom, https://go.uic.edu/DJinPractice"
ID 2: icons for ASL and captions with "Access info: CART and ASL will be provided. Contact dcc@uic.edu with any other access requests!" and a blurb (transcribed above).

@arabamcc @uicdiversity".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join us in celebration of National (and International) Week of Deaf People! We are delighted to welcome Deaf educator Marcos Aguilar for a Deaf culture installment of our series, Unlearning Ableism: DCC Discussions. How can we begin unlearning audism? What is audism, and what are some things we should understand about Deaf culture?

Unlearning Audism: Deaf Culture with Marcos Aguilar
Monday, September 23, 2024
3pm-4:15pm 
Daley Library 1-470 & on Zoom, https://go.uic.edu/DCCZoom

Co-sponsored by CASL (Club for American Sign Language)

About the presenter:
Marcos Aguilar (he/him), an avid adventurer, hails from Ecuador and moved to the United States around the age of seven. His thirst for knowledge is reflected in his educational pursuits, holding a Bachelor’s degree in Religious Studies from California State University, Northridge (CSUN) and a Master's in Sign Language Education (MASLED) from Gallaudet University. Spending around four to five years in Ecuador, he played a pivotal role in establishing the nation's inaugural Sign Language dictionary, demonstrating his commitment to enhancing the lives of Deaf communities.

Presently, Marcos Aguilar instructs American Sign Language (ASL) at Harper College in Palatine, Illinois, continuing his mission to uplift the quality of life within Deaf communities through education.

Audience note: This event is for everyone! All are welcome.

Covid safety information:
UIC does not require masking, but we are continuing to mask indoors as an accessibility measure for sick/immunocompromised folks and those living interdependently with them. Please wear a mask!* We’ll have extras on hand.

*If masking conflicts with your access needs, please check in with us! We can work something out.

Access information:
This presentation will be given primarily in ASL, with interpreters voicing the presenter. CART (live captions) will be provided. Room 1-470 is located on the first floor and is physically accessible. Please skip the cologne or perfume to make this a fragrance reduced environment! Contact dcc@uic.edu or 312-355-7050 with questions or to make any other requests.

[Image descriptions in the comments]".

Co-sponsored by CASL (Club for American Sign Language)

About the presenter:
Marcos Aguilar (he/him), an avid adventurer, hails from Ecuador and moved to the United States around the age of seven. His thirst for knowledge is reflected in his educational pursuits, holding a Bachelor’s degree in Religious Studies from California State University, Northridge (CSUN) and a Master's in Sign Language Education (MASLED) from Gallaudet University. Spending around four to five years in Ecuador, he played a pivotal role in establishing the nation's inaugural Sign Language dictionary, demonstrating his commitment to enhancing the lives of Deaf communities.

Presently, Marcos Aguilar instructs American Sign Language (ASL) at Harper College in Palatine, Illinois, continuing his mission to uplift the quality of life within Deaf communities through education.

Audience note: This event is for everyone! All are welcome.

Covid safety information:
UIC does not require masking, but we are continuing to mask indoors as an accessibility measure for sick/immunocompromised folks and those living interdependently with them. Please wear a mask!* We’ll have extras on hand.

*If masking conflicts with your access needs, please check in with us! We can work something out.

Access information:
This presentation will be given primarily in ASL, with interpreters voicing the presenter. CART (live captions) will be provided. Room 1-470 is located on the first floor and is physically accessible. Please skip the cologne or perfume to make this a fragrance reduced environment! Contact dcc@uic.edu or 312-355-7050 with questions or to make any other requests.

[Image descriptions in the comments]".
Account: UIC Disability Cultural Center.

posts.caption_raw:
"This week is Disability Voting Rights Week! 

Come check out our pop-up table at DCC for accessible voting locations, options, and accessible communications about voting. Check if you're registered and get registered if not! **

Chicagoland DPOCC and DCC will also be at UIC's Student Center East this Friday afternoon from 12pm-5pm. 

**if you or a friend/family member needs to Vote By Mail, the deadline to register in Illinois is October 31 👻[ghost emoji]

Organized by: 
Chicagoland Disabled People of Color Coalition, UIC's Disability Cultural Center, & the Institute on Disability & Human Development

#RevUpIllinois #RevUp #AAPD 
@idhduic 
@thisisuic 
@revupillinois

ID 3: Dante the DCC Program Coordinator has brown skin, yellow beanie and a green cardigan. She stands behind the Voter Registration table holding a sign sharing a student response to the "I Vote Because prompt."".

posts.caption_raw:
"Join us! We’re hosting an Open House and we’d love to connect with you. Meet our staff and learn what we’re up to this semester!

✨12-1pm (In person) Mingling & self-guided tours
✨1pm-2pm (In person & on Zoom) Welcome, DCC announcements & ways to get involved

The second half of the Open House will be hybrid, and we’ll say hi, hear from our collaborators, and share some highlights for the coming semester, including ways you can get involved!

Before that, we’ll be mingling with some drinks, pizza, and cupcakes on the 2nd floor outdoor patio of the Behavioral Sciences Building– you’re also welcome to venture inside and check out the disability activism exhibition and our Lounge spaces! (We’ll have gluten free, vegan, and GF+V options.)

This year, along with our sibling Centers, we’re celebrating the activism that gave rise to our Center. We’ll have a pop-up mini exhibit about disability activism at UIC for everyone to explore. 

💕 (heart emoji) All are welcome, come as you are. 

😷 (masked person emoji) Covid safety: UIC does not require masking, but we are still masking indoors as an accessibility measure for chronically ill / immunocompromised folks and those living interdependently with them. Please wear a mask in our spaces! We’ll have extras on hand. Zoom is another Covid-cautious option.

♿️ (accessibility emoji) Access info: Our Center is on the 2nd floor and physically accessible via elevator. The patio can be accessed via lift and has ramps connecting its various levels. We’ll have ASL interpreters available. For the hybrid portion, we will have CART captions projected into the space with the Zoom. Please get in touch with any access requests or questions at dcc@uic.edu or 312-355-7050!

Image descriptions in the comments!
@uicdiversity @thisisuic".
